data.title: General Dynamics 3Q Revenue Rises on Stronger Business-Jet Demand -- Update
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller


General Dynamics posted higher third-quarter revenue and profit, driven by increased orders for business jets as corporate profits continue to grow and a new tax law offered companies incentives to buy planes.

Sales rose 11% to $12.91 billion from the previous quarter, beating Wall Street's expectation of $12.5 billion. Top-line gains were fueled by a 30% increase in aerospace revenue, which Chief Executive Phebe Novakovic partially attributed to "very strong" order activity for business jets.

Seaport analyst Richard Safran said in a note last month that demand for business jets would likely continue to grow as President Trump's policies help grow corporate profits and a "super-wealthy class." Trump's tax bill signed earlier this year also allows businesses to deduct the full cost of a new or used business jet in the first year of acquisition. "We think that spurs increased demand from both existing and new customers," Safran said.

The U.S. government also has significant levels of unspent defense funding, which should offer a significant boost to General Dynamics' defense business as the Trump administration attempts to "pull-forward as much production as industry will facilitate," Safran said.

For the third quarter, the Reston, Va., aerospace-and-defense company on Friday reported net income of $1.06 billion, or $3.88 a share, up from $930 million, or $3.35 a share the year prior. Analysts had expected $3.71 a share, according to FactSet.

The company said its total estimated contract value was $167.7 billion at the end of the quarter, including a backlog of $109.9 billion.

Shares were up 2.1% to $348.55 in premarket trading.


Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-24-25 0748ET

data.title: Xali Gold Acquires Pico Machay Gold Project in Peru for US$17.5 Million
HCA Lifts Outlook as 3Q Profit, Revenue Rise
data.description:
By Connor Hart


HCA Healthcare lifted its full-year outlook after logging higher profit and revenue in the third quarter, as same-facility admissions grew.

The Nashville, Tenn., hospital owner and operation said earnings are now on track to come in between $6.5 billion and $6.72 billion, or $27 to $28 a share. It had previously guided for a profit of $6.11 billion to $6.48 billion, or $25.50 to $27 a share.

The company now expects revenue of $75 billion to $76.5 billion, up from its prior outlook of $74 billion to $76 billion. Analysts polled by FactSet had forecast earnings of $26.33 on revenue of $75 billion.

The new outlook came as HCA posted a profit of $1.64 billion, or $6.96 a share, for its three months ended Sept. 30, compared with $1.27 billion, or $4.88 a share, in the same quarter a year earlier.

Stripping out one-time items, adjusted earnings were also $6.96 a share. Analysts were looking for $5.72 a share.

Revenue rose 9.6% to $19.16 billion, topping the $18.57 billion that Wall Street modeled.

Same-facility admissions increased 2.1% during the quarter. Chief Executive Sam Hazen said the company is continuing its work to increase access to healthcare, invest in advanced technologies and train its workers.

Shares rose 2% to $449.05 in premarket trading.


Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

10-24-25 0748ET

Investors Expect Above-Target Inflation in Today's CPI Report
data.description:
Investors Expect Above-Target Inflation in Today's CPI Report By Vicky Ge Huang

In a rare move during a federal shutdown, the Bureau of Labor Statistics on Friday is set to release the consumer-price index for September. Another sharp increase is expected, which could push the rate of inflation to a 16-month high. But today's report isn't expected to sway the Federal Reserve from delivering an interest-rate cut next week. Elsewhere, Russia's central bank on Friday lowered its key interest rate for a fourth straight meeting as an already slowing economy braces for the impact of fresh sanctions from the U.S. and the European Union. And Turkey's central bank on Thursday slowed the pace of rate cuts at its latest meeting, after inflation came in higher than expected in September.

Top News Inflation Is Still Rising, CPI Likely to Show

The Federal Reserve is likely to cut interest rates next week for the second month in a row because it's worried about the U.S. jobs market. But it can't take its eye off rising inflation, either.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics on Friday is set to release the consumer-price index for September. Economists polled by The Wall Street Journal predict the cost of living will show an increase of 0.4% in September.

The more closely followed core rate, which omits food and energy, is forecast to rise a somewhat smaller 0.3%. Gas prices in particular can exaggerate inflation trends, so the core rate is a better measure of future inflation.

The rate of inflation in the 12 months ended in September, meanwhile, is expected to climb to 3.1% from 2.9% in the prior month. That would be the highest mark since the spring of 2024. (MarketWatch)

Russia's Central Bank Cuts Key Rate as New Sanctions Loom

The Bank of Russia cut its key rate to 16.5% from 17%

on Friday, having begun to lower borrowing costs from a recent peak of 21% in June. The move was smaller than previous cuts.

Turkey's Central Bank Slows Pace of Rate Cuts After Inflation Uptick

The Central Bank of Turkey cut its benchmark rate to 39.5% from 40.5% , it said Thursday. The bank previously cut rates by 2.5 percentage points and 3 percentage points in September and July, respectively.

BOJ Policy Should Align With Government Policy, Japan's FM Says

Japan's new finance minister doesn't see the need to comment on the direction of central bank policy , she said Friday, noting Bank of Japan Gov. Kazuo Ueda's commitment to supporting the economy by keeping monetary conditions accommodative.

Japan's Consumer Inflation Picks Up, But Rate Hike Timing Still Unclear

Commentary: Has Argentina Really Changed? Soon, We Will Find Out

The adage "The most dangerous words in investing on Wall Street are 'It's different this time'" could have been coined to describe Argentina. For 50 years, it repeatedly convinced international lenders it had changed its ways, only to slide back into default, instability and inflation.

This Sunday, Argentines will demonstrate whether this time really is different . Midterm elections will decide whether President Javier Milei's radical economic reform will continue, writes the WSJ's Greg Ip.

U.S. Economy Home Sales Rose in September, Boosted by Falling Mortgage Rates

Home sales rose in September to a seven-month high

after buyers seized on declining mortgage rates, offering some hope that the housing market could be improving. Sales of existing homes rose 1.5% from the prior month.

Trump Says He Is Terminating Trade Negotiations With Canada

President Trump said late Thursday he was terminating trade negotiations with Canada , pointing to a TV advert sponsored by the Ontario government that features the voice of Ronald Reagan as he speaks negatively about tariffs.

Financial Regulation Trump Pardons Convicted Binance Founder

President Trump has pardoned Changpeng Zhao , the convicted founder of the crypto exchange Binance, following months of efforts by Zhao to boost the Trump family's own crypto company.

The president signed the pardon on Wednesday, people familiar with the matter said. Trump recently indicated to advisers that he was sympathetic to arguments of political persecution related to Zhao and others, one of the people said.

Forward Guidance Friday (all times ET)

8:30 a.m.: CPI

9:45 a.m.: US Flash Manufacturing PMI

9:45 a.m.: US Flash Services PMI

10 a.m.: New Residential Sales

10 a.m.: University of Michigan Survey of Consumers - final

2 p.m.: Federal Reserve Board of Governors open meeting

Monday

8:30 a.m.: Advance Report on Durable Goods

10:30 a.m.: Texas Manufacturing Outlook Survey

Research U.S. Inflation Data, Global Rate Decisions Are Key For Dollar

U.S. inflation data on Friday and policy decisions from the Federal Reserve and other major central banks next week are key for the dollar's future direction, LMAX Group's Joel Kruger says in a note. The delayed inflation data for September will be critical ahead of the Fed's October 29 meeting where markets expect a 25 basis points rate cut, he says. "Global central banks, including the Bank of Japan, European Central Bank, Bank of Canada, and Reserve Bank of Australia are all under scrutiny as their policy decisions could influence dollar strength through interest rate differentials." U.S.-China trade tensions could also add volatility ahead of the APEC summit in South Korea, though a temporary truce is likely, he says. - Renae Dyer

Basis Points Retail trade in Canada continues to be choppy, with retailers facing a pullback in sales last month

that hints at spending uncertainty among consumers worried about job prospects, and fallout from a bruising trade dispute with the U.S. Business activity in Europe picked up in October , and continued to increase in Asia, indications that the global economy continues to show resilience in the face of higher tariffs and greater uncertainty about relations between powerful countries. U.K. retail sales unexpectedly rose in September , helped by tech stores and demand for gold. Retail sales volumes were 0.5% higher on month in September, marking a fourth-straight rise, following an increase of 0.6% in August, the Office for National Statistics said Friday. U.K. car manufacturing fell in September , taking a hit from the cyberattack that brought Jaguar Land Rover's operations to a standstill, an industry body said Friday. Asia-Pacific economies fared better than expected in the first half of the year but the IMF warns that the full blow of U.S. tariff hikes remains unclear , and growth will slow. About Us

WSJ Pro Central Banking brings you central banking news, analysis and insights from WSJ's global team of reporters and editors. This newsletter was compiled by markets reporter Vicky Ge Huang in New York. Send your tips, suggestions and feedback to [vicky.huang@wsj.com].

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today.


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

EU points to breaches of transparency obligations by Meta and TikTok
data.description:
The European Commission has announced that it has found preliminary evidence of breaches by TikTok and Meta of their transparency obligations under the Digital Services Act (DSA). In particular, the two platforms allegedly failed to provide researchers with adequate access to public data.

Meta, for Instagram and Facebook, is also alleged to be in breach of the requirement to provide simple mechanisms for users to report illegal content and challenge moderation decisions.

Henna Virkkunen, Executive Vice President for Tech Sovereignty, Security, and Democracy, emphasized that platforms must respect users' rights and be accountable in accordance with European law.

These findings are part of a series of investigations conducted by the Commission to ensure that large platforms comply with the requirements of the DSA.
